race: White (for example, English, German, Irish, Italian, Polish, Scottish, etc.)
Asian (for example, Chinese, Asian Indian, Filipino, Vietnamese, Korean, Japanese, etc.)
Hispanic or Latino (for example, Mexican, Puerto Rican, Salvadoran, Cuban, Dominican, Guatemalan, etc.)
Black or African American (for example, African American, Jamaican, Haitian, Nigerian, Ethiopian, Somali, etc.)

American Indian or Alaska Native (for example, Navajo Nation, Blackfeet Tribe of the Blackfeet Indian Reservation of Montana, Native Village of Barrow Inupiat Traditional Government, Nome Eskimo Community, Aztec, Maya, etc.)
Middle Eastern or North African (for example, Lebanese, Iranian, Egyptian, Syrian, Iraqi, Israeli, etc.)
education: Graduate or professional degree (MA, MS, MBA, PhD, JD, MD, DDS etc.)
relationship: Met my partner through the campaign
priorInvolvement: Attended a demonstration, march, rally, or protest.
Posted, shared, or created political content on social media.
Donated money to a political candidate or cause.
Contacted an elected official (email, phone, meeting)
Volunteered for a political campaign (canvassing, phonebanking, etc.)
Member of a political organization (e.g., DSA, county party, advocacy group)
Served in a leadership role within a campaign or political organization
Worked as paid staff on an electoral campaign or elected official’s office.
Worked at a 501(c)(4) organization advancing a political or civic cause
movements: Labor organizing ((strike support, contract campaigns, rank-and-file reform efforts)
Housing justice organizing (tenant union, anti-eviction organizing, Good Cause eviction campaigns)
orgs: Union (Unite Here!, UAW, UFT, DC 37, 32BJ, PSC CUNY, others
Jewish Voices for Peace Action or JFREJ
Muslim Democratic Club or other Muslim groups
heardAbout: Someone I know told me about the campaign
Zohran’s Hillside Ave / Fordham Road videos
Volunteer contacted you (knocked on your door, called you during phone bank, or other)
rank1: Negative feelings toward Adams, Cuomo, or Trump
top3: Negative feelings toward Adams, Cuomo, or Trump
reasonPoints: Negative feelings toward Adams, Cuomo, or Trump
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
repeatMotivations: I felt a strong commitment to the campaign’s mission or candidate.
I felt like my work was making a difference.
It became part of my regular routine.
I formed meaningful relationships with other volunteers.
I developed a sense of responsibility to the team.
I wanted to take on more leadership or responsibility.
Someone personally asked me to sign up again.
canvassDifficult: Asking voters to support the campaign.
canvassEasy: Using the canvassing app or materials
unsafeReasons: Gender-related safety concerns when canvassing alone.
keepGoing: Commitment to the cause or movement
A sense of responsibility to the campaign

canvassAgree.label: Canvassing pushed me outside my comfort zone
Canvassing helped me understand why I cared
Canvassing made me feel I could influence politics
Canvassing helped me develop new skills
Canvassing made me more confident talking politics

confidenceChange.label: Explain why I support a candidate

postCollab: Organized together in a political organization or group
Stayed in contact about politics but have not organized together
Worked on an issue campaign together
publicSignals: Posted about the campaign on social media
Told coworkers or classmates about the campaign
Displayed a yard sign or window sign
conversationRole: Sometimes the person who brought it up
Usually the person who brought it up
Usually responding when others brought it up
conversationOutcomes: I am not aware of any such outcomes
peopleSpokenWith: Stranger on the bus or subway
Stranger at a bar or club
Fellow member of club or association
outsideEncounters: Saw campaign posters or flyers in my neighborhood
Saw discussions about the campaign on social media
Overheard a conversation about the candidate or campaign
Discussed the campaign in a group chat

riskAgree.label: I stepped outside my comfort zone
I engaged in conversations I would have avoided
I tried to avoid situations where I might defend the campaign
I took on responsibilities I would not have accepted
I felt supported when I took political risks

sharedFate.label: When the campaign succeeded or failed, I felt it personally
We were building something larger than one election
I felt a shared sense of struggle
I felt responsible for the campaign's success

norms.label: I felt comfortable holding others accountable
It was clear what was expected of me
I was sometimes unsure what was expected
Clear how to take on greater leadership
The line between staff and volunteers was blurred

distributed.label: I felt empowered to initiate new ideas
When something needed to happen, I felt it was my place to act
I felt ownership over at least one part of the work
I looked to fellow volunteers for guidance
Volunteers were responsible for recruiting others

sinceCampaign.label: I follow local politics more closely
I talk politics with people I would not have before
I have taken on a political role or responsibility
I have attended a political event I would have skipped
I have encouraged someone else to get involved
I pay attention to neighborhood or city government
I consider myself part of a broader movement
I feel more connected to NYC

quotes.name: Ujji
quotes.text: After my coworker telling me about the prospective campaign I looked into Zohran's record and decided that it was the campaign for me.

activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: I had **previously promised myself I would never work on a campaign again** (I had volunteered for several and been officially employed by one and I felt just exhausted and disillusioned). The joy, practicality and honesty pulled me in, and I figured even if he couldn't win it would be a net positive for New York City to start to realize we don't have to elect the worst candidates.

quotes.name: Eleanor
quotes.text: I saw after moving here in July that Zohran was asking for people to start canvassing again for the general election (I would've canvassed in the primary if I had lived here). I knew I wanted to get involved in my new community right away and 3 hours outside on Sunday walking around my new home seemed like a pretty low-risk/entry-level option. I also had enjoyed canvassing in high school.

quotes.name: Evan
quotes.text: I decided before the forum I was going to volunteer once weekly for something Dsa

quotes.name: Elly
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: Applied for a job with Zohran and created a large doc on Cuomo opponent research and realized that we really have to elect Zohran over Cuomo. Also, saw a forum where Brad Lander wouldn’t pledge to not take money from Blackrock. At the time, Lander was favored to win.

quotes.name: Rex
quotes.text: He reached the matched donor fundraising cap then recorded a video saying "please stop sending me money and **donate me your time instead by canvassing**". I had **never seen this mess**age by any candidate in my entire political life and was instantly compelled to volunteer.

quotes.name: Chase
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: I had followed the campaign and been interested for a few months, but one day I was in the city already with nothing to do and realized I was able to get involved in Zohran’s campaign.

quotes.name: Logan
quotes.text: I was struggling to find work while preparing to return to grad school (August 2025) and decided I needed to take initiative and get involved in my local politics. I had already voted for Zohran in the primary, so he was my first stop.

quotes.name: Nikolas
quotes.text: I had a conversation with Zak Kazmi about becoming a field lead in my neighborhood, since we knew eachother from volunteering on a campaign in Jamaica together. He let me know that they were hosting field events in my neighborhood but that they needed people to lead them.

quotes.name: Brynn
quotes.text: I was reached out to in the fall of 2024 and asked if I wanted to help coordinate an early field launch for the campaign in December 2024. I took a moment to think it over, thought about whether or not I wanted to commit to that, and decided that I would make time for it.

quotes.name: Christopher
quotes.text:
I was watching MikefromPA from the Central_Committee Twitch stream and he mentions that a DSA member was running for mayor of NYC. He mentioned that he was a real socialist dedicated to advancing socialism and understood the problems and policy solutions. He even played this video of Zohran from the Gravel Institute https://youtu.be/LVuCZMLeWko?si=96PN7aerG2c9IwhJ

Which hammered home the point that Zohran was a true socialist.
As Andrew Epstein said, the left was hungry for a big project to go all-in on five years after Bernie and in the wake of the 2024 election.

quotes.name: Chase
quotes.text: I heard about the Mamdani campaign around Dec 2024/Jan 2025 through a former coworker, and while I had already planned to vote for him since then, it wasn't until May/June 2025 that I started volunteering. The second season of Andor came out in April 2025, and it completely changed my life. I was reminded of Nemik's manifesto in the show, about the need to keep trying in the face of oppression until we've finally won. I started having this overwhelming feeling that I needed to do whatever I could to make sure Mamdani won, because I realized I wouldn't be able to live with myself if my inaction led to Cuomo winning.

quotes.name: Laura
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: I’ve done outreach for a municipal animal shelter in the past (tabling on the streets and resource fairs) and I was good at talking to people on the streets. I’ve also always wanted to volunteer for a campaign like Bernie’s or AOC’s, but never had the time or capacity to do so. One day in early March, I opened up the Twitter (X) app and due to my algorithm, I saw Zohran’s Valentine’s Day video and thought the production of the video was so good, unlike anything I saw or heard before. So I checked out his socials, went to his website, read his proposals page and saw that he wants universal childcare, to freeze the rent, and fast and free buses—  I was automatically sold.

quotes.name: Silas
quotes.text: I graduated college in December 2024. I had wanted to get involved in organizing with DSA before then, but I never felt I had enough time. I really enjoyed my previous experiences with electoral organizing and I wanted to be a part of a campaign fighting for policies I believe in.

A couple friends who are more politically engaged than me were talking about the campaign and expressed excitement. I wasn't paying much attention to the race at that point, but I picked up on their feelings and decided that it was likely the most high-impact cause I could volunteer for at that moment.

quotes.name: Jared
quotes.text: I teach immigrants, and it has been a very scary and hard time for anyone in that community. I can't remember the exact moment I looked up how to volunteer, but it happend sometime after there was a scare of ICE potentially showing up to our school. Seeing an eloquently spoken and intelligent candidate speak up against the cruelty thats been prominent, especially in a non-capitulating manner, inspired me to take action. Watching our representatives surrender to far right authoritarianism time and time again is really demoralizing. Watching Mamdani actually fight (what should be the bare minimum) filled me with hope, and I wanted to share that.

quotes.name: Alex
quotes.text: I looked into Zohran when he surpassed all candidates other than Adams and Cuomo, hitting about 10% or thereabouts, somewhere in March 2025 I think. He had a good platform, was a talented communicator, and had some real leftist bona fides. At that point, I thought him actually winning the primary was a longshot, but decidedly possible. Once I believed that, I was compelled to volunteer, and get as involved as I could.

quotes.name: Emma
quotes.text: I **accidentally signed up** to volunteer for canvassing from an Instagram linked that a friend's cousin had posted. Somebody texted me (a real person, not a robot) to confirm my attendance. When I told them I couldn't make it, they offered me a few other options and I felt called to see it through. So I had them sign me up for a time that I could go after work.

quotes.name: Miriam
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: Went to dsa electoral forum where Z described how he decided to run, and org debated endorsing him

quotes.name: Andrew
quotes.text: I had understood from before DSA endorsed the campaign how large of an undertaking this would be and knew I would want to volunteer consistently at a field lead level. I tragically missed the first round of canvasses but I remember going to a winter social for the B/UM branch at Noches Mexicanes in the Bronx and having a wonderful time with people who i did not know and people who were new to democratic socialism. They called a great experience canvassing in China town and I knew I had to sign up. I quickly went to the next couple events in Wash Heights, which also had a lot of new people.

quotes.name: Ben
quotes.text: The DSA forum where Zohran first pitched his campaign. I knew of him as an assembly member but I was extremely impressed by his pitch.

quotes.name: Alex
quotes.text: The DSA town hall at the Unitarian Church in Downtown Brooklyn at the end of November 2024. I was craving some sense of political community and purpose after being disillusioned by Biden's and Harris's complicity in genocide, and devastated by Trump's re-election. I went in somewhat skeptical about Zohran, but was both convinced of the viability of his platform and of his path to victory in the mayor's race. From that point on, I became increasingly involved in the campaign, eventually becoming a field lead for Zohran and for Alexa Aviles (a DSA member of the City Council running for re-election), and rejoined DSA a few months later after having cancelled my paper membership in 2022.

quotes.name: Theresa
quotes.text: As a longtime DSA field person, I was asked to help on his petitioning field launch because we were getting more RSVPs than anticipated. This was in February or March of 2025. I canvassed for Zohran occasionally during the primary because I was busy with work (as an elementary school teacher) and grad school, including an election night poll site visibility double shift. I was a field lead for the general election campaign starting in the summer of 2025.

quotes.name: Ursula
quotes.text: in march, i heard people were collecting signatures and i have experience and didn't mind doing it

quotes.name: Nathaniel
quotes.text: I believe it was one of the ask videos during the primary. I had just moved to the city and while I had been fairly active in my union previously, since I was living in different city from where I was working I wanted to get involved in local organizing and this seemed like right option for the moment

quotes.name: Idana
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: I have volunteered for local NYC campaigns since high school with friends. Since I am at college now, I am further from home, but began emailing the campaign to organize student events for NYC residents that went to college in Boston/Cambridge once I saw a graphic of amount of individual donors in around February or March of 2025. Originally I heard about him polling at 2% in January or so and thought he never had a chance since the mayoral where Adams was elected I volunteered for, and it was disastrous, and I said I wouldn't volunteer for a mayoral again. In March I saw him get real traction and decided to start reaching out.

quotes.name: Daniel
quotes.text: It was his platform and ability to criticize capitalism and the genocide in Gaza.

quotes.name: Cara
quotes.text: I had **recently been broken up with** by someone I deeply cared about. He was a long time community organizer and I missed his intelligence, kindness, and adeptness at engaging large groups of volunteers/people he didn't know. I also felt stuck working in hospitality and that I would need a graduate degree to do anything else but couldn't afford one. Shortly after we broke up, my ex posted on social media about his involvement in the field operation often and I signed up, I believe, sometime in February - partially out of jealousy, partially out of grief, partially because Zohran's media presence seemed to have real traction - there was something solid about it, partically because people I resp…

quotes.name: Samuel
quotes.text: I was tracking the race before Mamdani announced and was considering whom I would/should volunteer for. When Mamdani announced, I liked his policy and charisma, but it wasn't until his first fundraising numbers that I saw he was a viable candidate that I fully decided to volunteer.

quotes.name: Sam
quotes.text: I attended a field lead training with a friend who had been an accountability buddy on previous campaigns, and what initially got me in was teaming up with her to do specific canvasses

quotes.name: Jimena
quotes.text: I can't remember an exact moment but I remember it felt like signing up and volunteering in the campaign was the only thing I could do to deal with my feelings of despair post Trump's win. I didn't think Zohran stood a chance to win when I signed up but I thought that it was a fantastic moment to organize other people who might feel a similar way and maybe build some local organizing muscle for future local and federal elections.

quotes.name: Nico
quotes.text: I graduated from college with a masters in public policy and was looking to get involved in the mayoral election.

quotes.name: Mat
quotes.text: My friend and I were **drunk at the bar** and I told them I was thinking about canvassing and they were like “I’ll come” and we went.

quotes.name: Francisco
quotes.text: I was asked to volunteer and I thought I needed to do something because of Trump so there I went to volunteer.

quotes.name: Daniel
quotes.text: I wasn't able to make December canvasses, so January was my first availability. Just came down to day and place.
I believe a text message from the campaign's SMS campaign persuaded me to go out and canvass, and made the process of signing up easy

quotes.name: Naomi
quotes.text: i was planning on moving to nyc to be closer to family and grandchildren and the timing felt auspicious....

quotes.name: Carrington
quotes.text: From the get-go a bunch of DSA members contributed the max 8-to-1 donation of $250 to give him the best chance possible. When the chapter endorsed him, many of us were all in from the start. Though still no one expected him to do as well as he did.

Growing frustration with not just  and trump but the veiling people were expressing me that everything is a foregone conclusion and there isn’t hope. I knew it was a long shot and when I first started volunteering, I just wanted to shoot for the moon and maybe move the political conversation. I’m glad we did that and so much more

quotes.name: Sisi
quotes.text: Zohran's **video about Tom Homan** put him on my radar, and I attended his DSA members only town hall. He convinced me that he could win. More importantly, he convinced me that he would do everything within his ability to win, rather than run a vanity campaign.

quotes.name: David
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: When I learned about his consistent stance on Palestine and his participation in a hunger strike

When I saw Zohran's 2024 video on Subway Takes talking about Eric Adams is making NYC more unaffordable, I knew that was a mayoral platform as soon as I saw it. Truly felt so galvanized in a way I hadn't felt since Bernie.

quotes.name: Madeline
quotes.text: No - after becoming aware of the campaign I volunteered as soon as there were slots available. I was so disillusioned and angry at Democrat leadership post-Trump reelection for utterly failing to meet the moment and for losing all trust and credibility with their refusal to acknowledge the situation in Palestine that it felt extremely urgent to support a candidate who was  in touch with reality, willing to engage on real issues, and not gaslighting voters.

quotes.name: Drew
quotes.text: I just wanted a way to do more than doom scroll, really liked Zohran, and really don’t like Andrew Cuomo or Eric Adams.

quotes.name: Kiyana
quotes.text: I’m a lesbian with **lots of trans friends**. Zohran loudly supporting trans people at a time when they were directly under attack from the Trump admin motivated me to get involved. I also had a friend who had been petitioning for Zohran, which gave me the confidence to show up and volunteer.
After seeing Zohran's videos on social media and donating, I decided if we want to see change in the City, I needed to get involved personally.

quotes.name: Kathy
quotes.text: I was trying to find my political home in NYC and had a conversation with a friend about doing it. I  said something like well if I hate it, I only have to do it once. That sentiment really pushed me to do it and be uncomfortable to try something new.

quotes.name: Anjali
quotes.text: I had become aware of Zohran's campaign and had donated to it but the idea of actually volunteering wasn't something I thought of. I **didn't think of myself as having the skillset** or the knowhow necessary to be effective at canvassing in the past, and I often didn't care enough about the political candidates (or rather, I never could fully agree with the ones who were running). In March of 2025 I saw some social media post about people volunteering who seemed "normal" (as in, not hyper aware of local politics, not particularly expert in the field etc.) and realized I might have the skillset after all.

quotes.name: Maddie
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: My friend told me about his campaign and we went petitioning together. It felt good to talk to people and create some community. The reason I got involved was his policy about pay parity for 3k and UPK teachers. I am a PreK teacher at a CBO with a DOE contract and we are paid so much less than teachers at DOE schools who serve the same kids. His approach to problems showed thoughtfulness around addressing the full problem—not just saying universal childcare but seeing what was necessary to achieve this.

quotes.name: Tatum
quotes.text: In 2024 I was applying for law school which took up all of my free time outside of my job, and I was increasingly losing faith in the Democratic establishment. The genocide in Palestine was eye-opening. As Ta-Nehisi Coates put it, I felt lied to. I thought that when Democrats talked about saving lives in Ukraine they meant it, and I didn't understand why the party supported death and destruction in Palestine. I graduated from Columbia in 2021 and remained in the neighborhood, so seeing my friends and former classmates get involved with the encampments helped open my eyes as well. I got involved lightly at the time, mostly going to protests in my neighborhood outside the school.

I was asked to sign up for the very first field lead training by Ravi (who’d I’d met through the Claire campaign and his AOC involvement) in January 2025 I believe? I knew I was going to be involved after hearing Z speak at the nycDSA convention at church of the village (I was there to sing with Sing in Solidarity), but had figured it’d just be as a regular volunteer, until I was asked.

quotes.name: Parker
quotes.text: I had a free spring break with nothing better to do, and so I decided I would spend it volunteering for Zohran Mamdani's mayoral bid. I had free time and set out to do something with it by working towards a cause I believed in.

quotes.name: Victoria
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: Brad Lander lost— the only option not to have Andrew Cuomo was Zohran, so he was the only person left to volunteer for.

I’d been seeing videos of him posted by friends and I thought the videos were both funny and deeply principled, but what really tipped me over into volunteering was that moment in the first debate when he was pressed about Israel. He could have caved, wavered, capitulated, equivocated, but he did none of those things. “I believe in a state with equal rights.” So principled, so succinctly passionate, so well-articulated, true… He communicated antizionist ideas into a distinctly American liberal vernacular in a way no one has done on the national stage in my lifetime. I was shook.

At Zohran’s town hall hosted by DSA in December 2024, I felt so empowered by the energy of the room and knew that something bigger than me was happening. I felt hopeful and excited to be surrounded by people who wanted to make New York better and for the first time in my life I thought it might actually be possible.

quotes.name: Michael
quotes.text: I had finished working on a comrade’s democratic socialist city council campaign during the primary and felt I could use my skills as a campaign worker to help the campaign once a week until the general. I got in one shift for Zohran before the primary but was occupied with the other campaign for most of my time, so when I finished my contract with her campaign I decided to spend more capacity on Zohran by volunteering weekly and helping get our movement over the finish line by trouncing Cuomo (who also happens to be one of the political figures I have fought against most in my career).

quotes.name: Charlotte
quotes.text: I hated Eric Adams's guts and wanted to at least help get someone else on the ballot against him

quotes.name: Allison
quotes.text: I knew I'd be involved in some capacity once we decided to endorse him, but I had been on a fence because I was worried about the difficulties of having a mayoral candidate and I was worried we might lose badly. I had helped a bit with petitioning and start canvassing weekly in late April. Not long after then the numbers really started to turn around for us and that's when I locked in, as soon as I thought we might actually win.

quotes.name: Magdalena
quotes.text: During the NYC-DSA endorsement period, Zohran called me to whip my vote for his endorsement. I told him I was already supportive and was especially excited about the buses. I pledges to be a day 1 donor, and was eventually asked to be in the campaign launch video. As soon as Zohran's endorsement passed I decided to dedicate myself wholeheartedly to the campaign. I decided to run for NYC-DSA electoral working group leadership because we were running Zohran. I decided I would commit myself to leading canvasses once a week (even though I hate knocking doors).

quotes.name: Maddie
quotes.text: signed up together with close friends for a canvassing session while at a friend's birthday party that had a sign-up table
A mixture of Cuomo announcing and petitioning ending. The first social media post after the petition ended.

quotes.name: Delaney
quotes.text: I wanted to volunteer for a mayoral candidate because I really didn’t want Cuomo or Adams to win. I considered Brad Lander but honestly, showed up to the Zohran canvass in January because it was easy to sit up and volunteer

quotes.name: Charlotte
quotes.text: I'm a DSA partisan, I'll do whatever the (proto-)party collectively decides! I abstained during the DSA endorsement vote because I wasn't convinced we had a serious shot at winning, but once he won the endorsement and once I saw his first video, I became convinced he would win, and signed up to be a field lead once I had the free time.

quotes.name: Katie
quotes.text: I've been following the campaign since February, and wanted to volunteer, however I hadn't been living in New York City yet so I didn't have a chance to do so. I mostly saw all the canvasses happening right before the primary and thought it looked super fun. When I got to NYC in August I decided to go to a canvass and became a lead shortly after.

quotes.name: Daniel
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: Was at a swimming pool in summer 2024 where a number of folks involved in his and other past campaigns talked about it.

quotes.name: Eric
quotes.text: I participated in the Brooklyn test canvass at the Brooklyn Museum — I was excited that we (DSA) had endorsed Zohran, and wanted to be part of the campaign!

I met an organizer justin. She probally doesn't even remember me, but I got to be trained up next to her, getting to learn about her long term fight, and the courage it takes to keep up the fight, and the powerful feelings she is fighting for. 

Thats when I realized, this is authentic, this is necessary, and I have a lot to say, and responsibility to listen and convince.

quotes.name: Jim
quotes.text: In December 2024 there was an effort from the nascent campaign field operation to "soft launch" canvassing in several different geographic areas as a test case for what turnout and enthusiasm would look like. As someone serving in NYC-DSA leadership with prior canvassing experience, but limited capacity at the time to become a full time field volunteer, I was asked to helped launch the first field event in my neighborhood--mostly in a senior support role for brand new, first-time volunteers who would ultimately be the full-time field leads.

quotes.name: Dara
quotes.text: I primarily canvassed in Kensington - a very polarized neighborhood with a large (pro-Zohran) Bangledeshi population, as well as a newer young professional/hipster/DSA type population (pro-Zohran) and a number of Hasidic Jews (very anti-Zohran). I was harassed personally and had canvassers harassed by Hasidic Jews. But one time we were launching a canvas and a 17 year old Hasidic kid showed up wanting to canvas - I sent him out with two exerienced canvassers (one wearing a Keffiyah) and he came back and had had the best time. We tried to give him merch but he said he'd only have to hide it at home. But he came back multiple times, and it was incredibly moving.

quotes.name: Eleanor
quotes.text: On the Monday before election day, I was paired with a new canvasser (young Muslim woman) on the Upper East side. Since it was ~3p or so, not many people were home. We went into a building across from the park where we launched and our first few conversations went well. However, after knocking on someone's door, we were asked how we got into the building, and when we said a delivery worker let us in, we were told to sit in the hallways and we were under "**citizen's arrest**" while he called the cops. I immediately contacted the field lead, and many informed us that citizen's arrest wasn't real in NYC, but this was a large man, and I felt unsafe leaving, especially with a young woman of color with me.

quotes.name: Rex
quotes.text: When canvassing in Harlem, I came across a doorman co-op that would not allow me entry despite my pleading. I then loaded MiniVan and came across a name in my voter list who had signed the petition for Zohran to appear on the ballot then cold-called him. He said he could not allow me into the building, but he did propose a separate offer of convening a town hall amongst his and neighboring co-op buildings to answer questions and pitch Zohran. We set a date and I reached out to the campaign for help, who connected me with someone more knowledgeable on policy.

quotes.name: Chase
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: I remember canvassing NYCHA housing buildings a few times in the Lower East Side, and a majority of people I spoke to were **Hispanic immigrants**. It was very common in these buildings for people to ask interrogatively who I was before they felt comfortable opening their doors, and to then be the nicest people in conversation. It took me a little while to realize it, but the early suspicion they held of me was because they were afraid I was ICE. That stuck with me, and it was very informative about just how terrifying it is to be an immigrant right now.

quotes.name: Logan
quotes.text: I will always remember GOTV week canvassing—at that point, everyone knew what our deal was, and it was more a matter of helping people take that final step to go out and actually vote. There were a few hostile encounters, but most people were polite and even happy to see us. It really made me feel like part of a movement.

Numerous working class people in **Soundview, the Bronx** reacting with the same **incredulity** a few days after the debate in which he was pressed on visit Israel.

Old Irish guys in Woodlawn, the Bronx knowing who he is and saying they would vote for him.

quotes.name: Chase
quotes.text: Near the end of the campaign, as early voting was starting to get tallied and we started seeing the exit polls, I think many of us started feeling a bit concerned that Cuomo might edge out Mamdani. In the final week, many others and I canvassed every single day leading up to election day, and what is most salient about that experience is how motivated everyone was to make sure Mamdani won. During the final weekend before election day, I canvassed and led teams from sunrise to sundown both days. It was exhausting, and it was starting to get colder outside, but like many others who kept coming back to canvass more, I knew I wouldn't be able to live with myself if I didn't do everything I could to make sure our guy won.

quotes.name: Laura
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: Like I mentioned earlier, I was already good at talking to strangers on the street about something I cared about. I’ve also always been publicly politically opinionated, discussing politics with friends, family, even colleagues. Zohran Mamdani’s mayoral campaign became a natural outlet for me to place my energy to. In the beginning, I felt anxious and nervous, but after a few tries, it became natural to me. After a canvassing shift, I felt energized, and that’s how I learned that I’m actually an extrovert. I learned that I’m actually really good at talking to other volunteers and managing other’s feelings and expectations. I think I honed in on a leadership role and I’m definitely a braver person today than before the campaign.

'- Speaking with a probable-Cuomo voter who seemed swayed when I ementioned that he takes money from the same donors as Trump.
- A man who answered the door in his underwear yelling at me.
- A family who probably went to vote mere hours before the deadline due to our canvas.

quotes.name: Jared
quotes.text:
a few things. 
1: I got to work with a **famous comedian** while canvassing.
2: People were so inspired that there were those who didn't live in nyc full time, or maybe moved out because they were outpriced, flying and commuting just to volunteer to help out.
3: I got canvassed on one of my off days.

quotes.name: Alex
quotes.text: While doing poll site visibility on primary day, I saw enthusiasm for voting, and for a political candidate that I did not believe was possible in the United States.

quotes.name: Andrew
quotes.text: On May 3rd in Hamilton Heights I did a solo turf that was probably the best canvass in my entire life. I didn't have an enormous amount of positive IDs but I spoke with a number of long-time Black co-op residents about Zohran's policies. These were mostly cuomo supporters who are usually the demographic that's hardest to win over to DSA. Residents were open to zohran but suspicious, and my presence there talking as someone living in the same neighborhood felt key to opening up a real conversation about problems in our society.

quotes.name: Ben
quotes.text: Just to elaborate on feeling unsafe, I had one incident where I felt unsafe where a voter tour up my campaign lit, cursed and yelled at me, **called me an anti semite (I’m Jewish!)** and told me to never come back to his neighborhood again.

quotes.name: Alex
quotes.text:
During the last days before the primary election, I was knocking doors during an evening shift on a hot night in Sunset Park. I was standing on a doorstep hoping somebody would answer and looking down at the street below me. I saw two neighbors, one Hispanic and one Chinese, wave to each other. They started speaking to each other haltingly, in each other's languages. The former yelled out "ni hao" to his neighbor, who promptly replied that it was "muy caliente" that evening. 

This stood out to me because the campaign cycle had been dominated by fearmongering and hateful rhetoric from our opponents, and to see this simple exchange heartened me and made me remember what we were fighting for--namely, the right of the working class of this polyglot city to live and coexist in comfort and frie…

quotes.name: Theresa
quotes.text: On election night in November, i spoke with a Spanish-speaking voter and told her there were only a couple hours left. She put on her shoes and left immediately. That made me really happy.

quotes.name: Ursula
quotes.text: A whole building of zohran supporters and they'd **rigged the whole building to play to call to prayer** **just as we knocked** on the door and it was so meaningful to see that a community had built a haven for themselves.

quotes.name: Nathaniel
quotes.text:
At the door: I had a long conversation with an older man in Staten Island (we were canvassing in Port Richmond) who at first pretended he didn't know who Zohran was and then after we talked to him and listened for a long time he was really excited about the platform, and asked for a bunch of fliers to give to his sons. 

Overall: as a field lead, the main experience that stay with me are all the debrief conversations I had with canvassers, many of whom felt they were "doing something political" for the first time in their lives. They wanted to talk through their feelings and experience and I was very honored to do that with them.

quotes.name: Gabriela
quotes.text: During my second canvass I had a long conversation with a middle-aged woman who was very moved by the campaign's message and offered me a glass of water. It was really special to make an immediate connection with someone I probably would not have met otherwise, through a shared commitment to supporting our neighbor's basic needs. This became a regular occurrence that has made me feel more connected to my community overall.

quotes.name: Idana
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: I have been canvassing since I was around 15, and I am 21 now, but I remember field leading in the Upper West Side and training one of my friends who is Jewish and lives up there. She got very antagonistic with another Jewish voter who hated Mamdani, and would be very open to Jewish people about her pro-Mamdani stance and would be extremely anti-Cuomo. It was difficult to tell her that was not our job -- we can't convince everyone, and I found it very hard to both dissuade random people from harassing me and following me around for being pro-Mamdani on my shifts, and also tell volunteers that they can walk away from conversations as well.

quotes.name: Cara
quotes.text: I trend skepitcal so I have a hard time giving motivational speeches. I prefer to focus on logistics and outcome, but I remember leading one of my last "regular" canvasses before GOTV for the primary - It was a big group, some of the volunteers I had seen weekly, a lot were new and I had been leading canvasses for a couple months at that point. I decided to try a motivational speech and I said something true and I tried to say it with some sort of gusto and sincerity. I believe I said, "before you guys go out, I'm going to tell you something another lead said to me which at the time, I fully did not believe, but now I have come to believe and it is that, doorknocking wins elections. What you are about to go do in the next three hours is help win this election.

quotes.name: Samuel
quotes.text: I had the privilege to once canvass with **Mira Nair, Zohran's mom**. We knocked on the door of a family who was Muslim. They were so **proud of Zohran**'s representation of their faith and told us about how they could not wait for municipal grocery stores to open because they often have to decide between paying rent and buying food. Despite their mention of groceries being unaffordable, they tried to send Mira and me home with bags of oranges as a thank you for the conversation.

quotes.name: Sam
quotes.text: I was in the midst of a lot of not homes/kind of hostile territory in this lower income housing complex in Queens when I came upon this young black woman who took the flyer and said "you know, I think I will vote for him, it's time to givesomeone else a try " and in many ways that sort of summed up what this campaign was all about

quotes.name: Jimena
quotes.text:
Early days in my first canvass as a lead, I talked to a young **Orthodox guy** about the campaign, he said he'd recently **registered to vote** for the November election and he was actually incredibly receptive about the campaign goals. I reminded him to vote in the primary. And then he asked if he could vote for Zohran as a registered Republican.

To me it was such a powerful moment of how broad and inclusive the message was and how it could actually bring way more people into the fold that wouldn't consider themselves leftists or socialists at all.

quotes.name: Nico
quotes.text: Just the overall experience of talking with someone that actually knew the candidate. I have done plenty of canvassing for lesser known candidates and the conversations are much less fruitful.

quotes.name: Francisco
quotes.text: An older black woman on saturday was privately questioning her and her friends support for Cuomo. On Sunday after church I went to her apartment and talked to her about zorhan. She jumped for joy because I came to her door to talk about someone she was considering to vote for. She took a lot of lit to give to her friends and said that God sent me to her door to ask her to vote for zorhan.

quotes.name: Daniel
quotes.text:
More an overarching experience, but getting to see my weekly **Bay Ridge canvasses grow** and the **crew sustain itself over time**, and growing as a lead along with them. 

Also primary day, which was a wild day I'll never forget (so hot out, ugh)

Many of my fellow canvassers were born in NYC. While I grew up within commuter rail distance of the city, it struck me that the people who care most about the city are those who are from here and plan to live here forever, and that more work needs to be done convincing transplants to take part in local politics to the benefit of working class people.

quotes.name: Naomi
quotes.text: Really enjoyed talking with other volunteers who had come to nyc for vacation from around the world and to their surprise ended up working on the campaign

quotes.name: Carrington
quotes.text: Early in the campaign, Zohran's canvassing strategy was to consolidate voters in areas where he was strongest. I wanted to spread the good news in more neglected areas and went out to East New York, Canarsie, Cypress Hills. Talking to young black men there who naturally struggled with cynicism. Them wanting to believe something good might be possible, not letting themselves fall for it, but then listening really hard. Them telling me, "Tell Zohran the youth need jobs."

quotes.name: Sisi
quotes.text: I was doing door to door and poll site visibility in Astoria on the primary Election Day. I helped an elderly man figure out who he should vote for in the down ballot elections that nobody really pays attention to. He told me that one day I will be older and I'll think back to the time that I spent my youth doing this in 100 degree weather.

quotes.name: David
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: I went to one man's house in Staten Island looking for three voters, none of whom were home. He was working on a car and the yard. Yard. He had a strong accent from an African country. He said the three people I was looking for were his kids and that they were out canvassing too. He however could not vote because of his immigration status.
Easily being in a big Bay Ridge building and speaking to two older couples who were super excited for Zohran in a way that shocked me but made me so excited.

quotes.name: Madeline
quotes.text: There are too many to count. When I walk the streets of my neighborhood now, I remember the conversations I had at many of the doors I pass—the good, the bad, and the awkward. But mostly good. Sometimes sad. I remember a woman at Gowanus Houses telling me about the costs she was drowning in as she tried to care for her elderly neighbor and pay for her own healthcare. There was so little I could do, but I could listen. I remember the former Dinkins staffer who was so energized that I had showed up at her door, she offered to send an email out to her old coworkers urging them to support the campaign. A mom who came downstairs to talk to me while her toddler was actively crying on her hip because she saw the value of having a conversation with a neighbor.

quotes.name: Drew
quotes.text: I will never forget on the night of the last day to vote in the June primary election, I buzzed someone’s door and told them who I was with. They wanted to talk to me, so they came downstairs and met me on the sidewalk. We talked for about ten minutes about how he considered himself progressive but was worried that Zohran wouldn’t be tough enough to stand up to Trump but that Cuomo maybe would be. I talked to him about how I believed differently, including that Cuomo’s campaign was being funded by the same billionaires that put Trump in office. Eventually he was convinced, and he said he was going to vote for Zohran. I think about that moment a lot as being a major turning point for me, and I will always remember it.

quotes.name: Kiyana
quotes.text: Learning how to distill a message and tailor that message to the person I’m speaking to based on the concerns/problems they express

quotes.name: Kathy
quotes.text: My first canvassing experience was with a **90+ year old black woman** in Brooklyn. This was in January 2025, so **no one knew who Zohran was** (especially in Brooklyn) and we discussed Eric Adams and then Zohran and his policies. She was extremely disappointed in Eric Adams and was not hopeful for the future of the city for upcoming generations. She was bed ridden due to her age and had a caretaker full time. She also lived in a rent stabalized apartment that she'd lived in for decades. After we discussed Zohran, she thanked us for coming by and commented on the importance of what we were doing. She had been alive during a time when she did not have the legal right to vote, so she now makes a point to vote in every single election, no matter what.

quotes.name: Tatum
quotes.text:
I was a field lead on the Upper West Side where volunteers encountered both significant support and opposition to the campaign. Some of the most negative encounters stick with me as experiences that opened my eyes to Islamophobia, but what sticks with me much more was the resilience of volunteers. 

On several canvassing shifts I helped volunteers process negative encounters that anyone might understandably stop canvassing after. I emphasized that they did not need to push themselves past their comfort zone and had done more than enough if they felt like calling it quits. But every single time I talked to a volunteer who had an experience like this they ended up going back out and finishing their list. That resilience speaks to how much we believed in the work, in DSA, and in Zohran.

Honestly it felt like we were barometers of media coverage’s effects more than the ones influencing voters - I just remember over time seeing more people know who he was (vs the beginning of canvassing when nobody knew), and thinking “thank you Zohrans comms team for doing the work for us”. Persuasion feels like an illusion sometimes - it’s more like our existence alone was proof to people he had a chance more so than what we said (it was mainly older voters who were the most openly saying “he can’t do any of those things he promises” vs younger voters who wanted to believe he could)

quotes.name: Wahi
quotes.text: I was 16 at the time, and field leading. I met a 15 year old canvasser, who has now become one of my closest friends, and we also made Teens4Zohran on Instagram together.

Canvassing for Zohran changed my life. When I think about it, I literally get teary. Talking to every day New Yorkers about the material issues that affect, talking to them across huge ethnic material political and cultural differences, finding solidarity in collective struggle… this is the stuff that is out of our phones, that is relational, connective, true… Canvassing for Zohran was one of the more human, connective things I’ve ever experienced in my life. And I never felt dirty or bad about it because I 100% believed in this candidate. I would never be able to say that about any machine politician that won’t explicitly call themselves a socialist, focus on class politics over culture politics, and stand for Palestinian liberation without equivocation.

Knocking on the door in a rent stabilized Flatbush building of an older Black gentleman who said he had not voted in a long time because he had been so disappointed in politics. He was really interested in Zohran’s platform, told us about his struggles with affordability and rent, and explained his distrust in politicians like Eric Adams. We told him where he could vote in June (he didn’t know his poll site despite living there for years) and he seemed interested in doing so in June. He was very happy to have spoken to me and my canvassing partner. I left feeling very fulfilled.

quotes.name: Michael
quotes.text: I had a conversation with a middle- aged Black woman living in rent-stabilized housing who had not previously heard of Zohran. She was initially going to vote for Cuomo because of name recognition, but I convinced her otherwise. When she heard about Zohran’s rent freeze she seemed a little skeptical it was possible, but when she heard about Cuomo’s contributions from and ties to the real estate industry and landlord lobby, she saw that as corrupt and against the interests of tenants. By using my experience facing off against Cuomo in the 2019 HSTPA fight as a staffer for Julia Salazar, I was able to convince her to vote for Zohran as a candidate who will support tenants, not landlords.

quotes.name: Allison
quotes.text: So many experiences of people already being excited about the campaign, but especially any time a fellow DSA member would answer the door it would make me so happy

quotes.name: Magdalena
quotes.text: I don't think I'll forget my first ever Zohran canvass. December 14, 2024 in Astoria. We were exclusively canvassing rent stabilized buildings. We had a conversation with a woman who had just moved into her apartment. She had never heard pf Zohran before. Once she heard Zohran would freeze her rent, she was sold. We listened to what she cared about and how much she hated Eric Adams. She even donated right there! Never had I gotten a donation at the door before or after that canvass ever again. That conversation made me realize this campaign would be very different from anything we had ever done before.

quotes.name: Maddie
quotes.text: Spoke with a **Bushwick resident who voted for Trump** about the cost of living crisis, and support for unhoused community members, and **she committed to voting for Zohran** by the end of the conversation (and to asking her nine children to do the same).

quotes.name: Delaney
quotes.text: I once saw an apartment door with signs that indicated the person was more conservative (blue lives matter) but then when he opened the door, he was such a strong supporter. It reminded me that this campaign went beyond typical lines and that we could really reach people’s wouldn’t think we could reach

quotes.name: Alan
quotes.text: In the weeks leading up to the primary I was canvassing all over Brooklyn and older voters and people of color were excited about Zohran and even giving shout outs in the streets where earlier in the campaign it seemed only whiter and younger voters were familiar with him. That was when I knew he had broken out of his social media millienial containment into the wider public and felt confident that he was on track to win.

quotes.name: Katie
quotes.text: Towards the end of the campaign, I had one of my regular canvassers come up to me, and he told me that he was extremely nervous the first time he came out to knock doors, and wasn't sure if it was for him but that our enthusiasm and support as leads made him come back out over and over again and fall in love with canvassing. It really stayed with me because it showed how much impact one person can make and how important this joyful and hopeful attitude was that really characterized this campaign and set it apart from others.

quotes.name: Eric
quotes.text: During a Sunset Park shift, I spoke to a woman who ran a **daycare primarily for the children of immigrants**. She told me about how the families all had **go bags** (the kids didn't know what they were), and how the children would often tell her they loved her before leaving because they didn't know if they would be back the next day. It was one of the few times I was truly speechless canvassing — just an appalling thing to hear how afraid so many of our neighbors were, and it made me even more committed to building a safer and more just New York.

quotes.name: Jim
quotes.text: Still early in the primary campaign (perhaps March or April?) I was field leading, and we always asked attendees to introduce themselves and share if it was their **first time canvas**sing.  I pretty quickly clocked a volunteer who seemed very nervous to be there and decided I would walk with her to model the process. As we walked and talked, she shared some of her anxieties about talking to strangers in general and about politics specifically. She also shared that the social media and video component of the campaign, and the specific emphasis on showing regular people volunteering, gave her confidence to step out of her comfort zone and come to this event.

quotes.name: Dara
quotes.text: This was the best run campaign I've ever been a part of. The community it built was warm and supportive, and you felt it throughout the city. There was a sense of community and a love for NY that really was palpable everywhere I went. And even though I am not very merch driven as a field lead - the merch was KEY. There was a whole merch economy.

quotes.name: Eleanor
quotes.text: This campaign set me up for success in NYC. Even despite my crazy experiences, I felt I had something to connect with others over, and I know it inspired my family in their own communities.

quotes.name: Chase
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: I’m a poli-sci student at Rutgers in New Brunswick and I grew up in New Hampshire, so politics has always been part of my life.

quotes.name: Logan
quotes.text: Volunteering on the campaign has encouraged me to apply to broader public service/local government jobs than I was before. I feel like a stronger leader and more prepared for that kind of work in the future.

quotes.name: Christopher
quotes.text: I joined because of the socialist vision and to expand the socialist movement in NYC and in the US. During my time as a lead, I always ended each canvass asking volunteers to consider joining DSA and learning more about socialism.

quotes.name: Chase
quotes.text: Volunteering changed my life. It has completely reoriented my own value system as well as my place in broader society. It taught me the value of community based on common goals rather than on heritage or tradition. Since the campaign, I have become much more involved in local community efforts, such as food pantry distribution and other mutual aid services. Mamdani's campaign showed me what was possible when we live in a city unified by hope rather than divided by fear

quotes.name: Laura
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: I often say that Zohran’s campaign changed my life, its made me a better communicator, open to have hard conversations with people I may disagree with, I feel more connected with my neighbors and city at large, I’m more brave and confident in my abilities. I am now volunteering my time with a congressional campaign for NY13, where I’m assuming a bit more leadership responsibilities along other volunteers I met during the Zohran campaign. Organizing has been a great way to connect with other politically minded people and it feels like a big ongoing project for a better world.
I tried to avoid emotional attachment to the outcome of the election, but I stayed committed to showing up for my part consistently.

quotes.name: Jared
quotes.text: I cannot express how much hope was involved in this campaign. At a certain point it wasn't even about Mamdani, but what he represented. After everything thats happened since Trump was elected, it felt like having my head held under water then suddenly having someone take the weight off my head, and I could sit up and breathe finally. I know that the work is far from over, I know the dangers of making one man a demigogue, I'm sure Mayor Mamdani would agree. But it's about so much more than one person. Honestly, it's about so much more than one city. It's about all of us. It's about the future. It's hope. And isn't that what it means to be an American?

quotes.name: Alex
quotes.text:
I think it's important to remember that the campaign was built on a blueprint forged by DSA in NYC and elsewhere. What was new was that it was a winnable campaign for a big seat with national attention, though the Zohran campaign specifically did create a lot of that "winnability" through strategy and their actual platform. 

While I differ philosophically from DSA, and even from electoralism somewhat, one must place credit where credit is due. This was a clear chance to radically improve local government by electing someone who was not beholden to wealthy and corporate donors. That was enough for me to get involved, and enough for a lot of people to vote.

quotes.name: Andrew
quotes.text: it's all about the work layed by DSA for the last ten years. that's what has enabled the expansive volunteer model zohran built.

quotes.name: Alex
quotes.text: Clarification on two previous questions: I put "no" for whether I pay closer attention and whether I took a job in public service after the campaign only because I work for a NYS legislator, and thus was already doing both things before volunteering for Zohran.

quotes.name: Sam
quotes.text: I was sort of hoping to come out of this campaign with a better sense of what made it successful. I remember many politically-aligned people rolling their eyes a bit at the "let's knock one million doors" focus, but didn't really see a clear alternative of what volunteers could do to help the campaign succeed. While I had one or two conversations at doors that felt like meaningful persuasion, canvassing felt more like an excercise in polling particular neighborhoods at different times and sometimes watching them change from "never heard of the guy" to "oh I'm totally supporting him".

quotes.name: Mat
quotes.text: This wasn’t my first rodeo and I’ve lived in New York long enough that I wasn’t looking for friends (made some anyways, but not in the way it was important like it was for other volunteers). Also, I was often the oldest guy at the canvass (not that old at 45 lol).

quotes.name: Daniel
quotes.text: I have been involved in political campaigns (candidates and issues alike) and organizing for about 20 years, and the Zohran campaign was easily the most impactful experience I've had on a campaign. From helping build cultures and systems from the ground up, to empowering others and being empowered, to the ease of use when it came to campaign systems and tech, to a message I could get behind, they all aligned on Zohran's campaign. It was truly an experience I'll never forget and hopefully through Our Time, DSA, JFREJ, WFP, and other campaigns and groups I'll continue to grow my skills and maintain my communities.

quotes.name: Naomi
quotes.text: Being decades older than most of the volunteers and staff members I encountered, I was really glad to find a camaign that drew so many different ages from such varying backgrounds...

quotes.name: Carrington
quotes.text: Some of the questions are worded funny. Eg, have you been more involved in politics since working on Zohran campaign. The answer is 'no' because I've stayed the same amount involved, which is quite a bit. The whole previous page or questions was all no's because I was already doing most of those things.

quotes.name: Madeline
quotes.text: Actively looking for the next campaign to get involved in… will likely pick up a shift or two for Brad Lander but would love to put my canvassing skills to use for another leftist, pro-Palestinian candidate who actually seems attentive/responsive to voter needs and willing to do the hard work of governing.

quotes.name: Kiyana
quotes.text: It was a powerful lesson in people powered movements. Money backed campaigns are daunting, but there are more workers than there are ultra wealthy individuals. We have collective power. It also helped clarify my own political ideology. I realized I was very much a socialist, even though I probably would have been afraid to use that label previously.

quotes.name: Maddie
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: It was a really great experience. I think towards the end when there were so many new field leads I was a bit worried some of them did not have enough training. For the general election it was hard to keep up the motivation to continue leading 2 times a week. I also think there could have been more direct announcements for leads. A few times campaign policies would be said only in a zoom meeting so some leads would not know the new policies.

quotes.name: Tatum
quotes.text:
The work I did on the Zohran campaign was the best work I had ever done in my life. The campaign turned me into an organizer and transformed me as a person. My life is unrecognizable before and after it. 

I knew from the beginning that I wanted to be part of something larger than one election, so I joined NYC-DSA at the same time that I started volunteering. I was stunned to learn at my DSA 101 in December 2024 that the organization was 100% volunteer run. (Since then two staff members have been hired.) I'm now one of those volunteers, dedicating all of the free time I can find, about 20 hours a week, to the organization. I hold an elected position in a working group and in a caucus.

The hardest part about being a field lead was dealing with a reoccurring canvasser who was on the spectrum/had obvious learning disabilities, who made various remarks that made (especially) women uncomfortable, but the campaign refused to ban him from canvassing (I think each borough had canvassers like this). As a field lead it was stressful enough to get everyone sorted - to have to babysit another person on top of it was a lot sometimes. But I never wanted to (& still never want to) get paid for doing any volunteering - it would corrupt the sanctity of the act.

quotes.name: Victoria
activities: Host a staging location or materials hub
quotes.text: I think this survey doesn’t recognize lots of us have been doing this work forever and Zohran just happens to be the candidate now.

I just became so **urbanism-pilled** I read The Power Broker after Zohran won, lol. It awoke in me this fierce level of NYC patriotism I didn't even think was possible. And it also jolted me out of just being an **armchair socialist / black-pilled Chapo-listener** to an active DSA political organizer now. I will be forever grateful to that man and his campaign team for that. Zohran changed my life.

quotes.name: Michael
quotes.text: As a committed socialist and DSA member since 2017, it was an important milestone after 10 years of electoral and community organizing that laid the foundations for his victory. I saw comrades from past campaigns leading countless more new volunteers and new DSA members in their political development and engagement.
The **secret sauce to canvassing was the socials afterward**. It **can't be just a lit drop off**. You don't get that with paid canvassing.

quotes.name: Delaney
quotes.text: I think I often shied away being very vocal about specific candidates because I don’t feel like I can trust them. This campaign felt different because it felt bigger than Zohran. It was less about a guy and more about a movement. It also helped that I knew he wasn’t bought like most other candidates.

quotes.name: Alan
quotes.text: After the campaign I was recruited to run for the Organizing Committee (leadership) of North Brooklyn DSA and I came in 2nd out of 7 people running for 5 seats. I was also recruited by New Kings Dems, Christian Celeste Tate (DSA candidate for Assemblymember in District 54) and Councilmember Sandy Nurse to run for State Committee/District Leader in Assembly District 54, which I am currently doing.

quotes.name: Jim
quotes.text: The campaign confirmed many hopes and theories I previously held about electoral work and movement organizing at a scale I never imagined possible.  I have a level of confidence about movement building--not in the abstract, but in specific, strategic ways--that I did not have before this campaign. But mostly, as someone who was already in leadership and organizing on the left prior to the campaign, it was extremely affirming and powerful to see so many other people personally transformed by the experience of participating with agency and ownership in a shared political project for the first time.

hero.name: Alex
hero.text: The DSA town hall at the Unitarian Church in Downtown Brooklyn at the end of November 2024. I was craving some sense of political community and purpose after being disillusioned by Biden's and Harris's complicity in genocide, and devastated by Trump's re-election. I went in somewhat skeptical about Zohran, but was both convinced of the viability of his platform and of his path to victory in the mayor's race. From that point on, I became increasingly involved in the campaign, eventually becoming a field lead for Zohran and for Alexa Aviles (a DSA member of the City Council running for re-election), and rejoined DSA a few months later after having cancelled my paper membership in 2022.

hero.name: anon
hero.text:
I’ve lived in New York City since I was 18, and I’ve been in the city for 13 years. I’ve always loved New York and have always planned on spending the rest of my life here, but being a part of this campaign deepened my love for this city in a way that I can’t even fully put into words because it’s so powerful an emotion. Loving the chaos, the diversity, the love of your fellow neighbor, the solidarity in struggle, the history, the sweaty grittiness of it all, it made me love this city in a way I didn’t even think was possible. I read the power broker after Zohran won lol because I just became so urbanism-pilled. It awoke in me this fierce level of NYC patriotism that I didn’t even think was possible. And it also jolted me out of just being an armchair socialist / black-pilled Chapo-listener to an active DSA political organizer now. I will be forever grateful to that man and his campaign team for that. Zohran changed my life.

SOCIALISM FORVER!!!!!!! NYC FOREVER!!!!!!!!!! SOLIDARITY FOREVER!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

hero.name: Jim
hero.text: Still early in the primary campaign (perhaps March or April?) I was field leading, and we always asked attendees to introduce themselves and share if it was their first time canvassing.  I pretty quickly clocked a volunteer who seemed very nervous to be there and decided I would walk with her to model the process. As we walked and talked, she shared some of her anxieties about talking to strangers in general and about politics specifically. She also shared that the social media and video component of the campaign, and the specific emphasis on showing regular people volunteering, gave her confidence to step out of her comfort zone and come to this event. As we canvassed for close to 3 hours, I watched this person turn from very apologetic and apprehensive, to quite confident in herself and willing to do entire floors of doors alone.  This person ended up coming back frequently for canvasses. I had abstractly believed there could be a transformative component to this particular strategy and process, but I hadn't previously seen it happen so acutely in the course of one day.

hero.name: Cara
hero.text: I trend skepitcal so I have a hard time giving motivational speeches. I prefer to focus on logistics and outcome, but I remember leading one of my last "regular" canvasses before GOTV for the primary - It was a big group, some of the volunteers I had seen weekly, a lot were new and I had been leading canvasses for a couple months at that point. I decided to try a motivational speech and I said something true and I tried to say it with some sort of gusto and sincerity. I believe I said, "before you guys go out, I'm going to tell you something another lead said to me which at the time, I fully did not believe, but now I have come to believe and it is that, doorknocking wins elections. What you are about to go do in the next three hours is help win this election." I couldn't really look at anyone while I was saying it but after I did, I looked up and saw probably 10 pairs of eyes that had become a lot brighter than they were 30 seconds prior (I think!) and I knew that I was doing the thing I was supposed to do as a lead which was motivate people, and I was doing it not because I like to (if its not clear I really hate doing that sort of thing) and it worked and I was able to give people something that was given to me which was, I think, a real sense of purpose and a real sense of agency; not for the rest of their lives, not even for the next three hours probably, but just in that moment and that was a really really good feeling. Probably the best feeling.

hero.name: Tatum
hero.text:
The work I did on the Zohran campaign was the best work I had ever done in my life. The campaign turned me into an organizer and transformed me as a person. My life is unrecognizable before and after it. 

I knew from the beginning that I wanted to be part of something larger than one election, so I joined NYC-DSA at the same time that I started volunteering. I was stunned to learn at my DSA 101 in December 2024 that the organization was 100% volunteer run. (Since then two staff members have been hired.) I'm now one of those volunteers, dedicating all of the free time I can find, about 20 hours a week, to the organization. I hold an elected position in a working group and in a caucus. 

I wish everyday I could put more of my time and energy into organizing. I'm now in law school seeking a degree that can provide me some stability in a future job. I hope to transition to a role in politics someday -- something I never would have contemplated before this campaign. I've had opportunities to interview for jobs in the DSA orbit since the campaign ended but a crucial issue is that they can rarely guarantee more than a handful of months of employment. 

In addition to affecting my career and life trajectory, the Zohran campaign helped me develop an enormous number of skills. I improved at public speaking, managing large groups, delegating tasks, having difficult conversations, and making organizing asks. 
 
A final note is that I think two macro events had some influence on the availability of "super volunteers" (those of us who were basically treating this like a job by April/May/June 2025) -- the Affordable Care Act and Trump's cuts to grant funding and programs like PeaceCorps and AmeriCorps. Many of us who could dedicate the most time to volunteering were unemployed. I quit my job about a month earlier than I had planned so that I could dedicate more time to it, and I was able to do that because I could get health insurance through the ACA. I talked to a number of volunteers who had more time available because programs they were in through the federal government had funding cut or were completely ended. I think the availability of health insurance and the sudden drop off of opportunities for recent college graduates who wanted to "do good" contributed to the availability of volunteers.

hero.name: Rex
hero.text: He reached the matched donor fundraising cap then recorded a video saying "please stop sending me money and donate me your time instead by canvassing". I had never seen this message by any candidate in my entire political life and was instantly compelled to volunteer.

hero.name: Dara
hero.text: I primarily canvassed in Kensington - a very polarized neighborhood with a large (pro-Zohran) Bangledeshi population, as well as a newer young professional/hipster/DSA type population (pro-Zohran) and a number of Hasidic Jews (very anti-Zohran). I was harassed personally and had canvassers harassed by Hasidic Jews. But one time we were launching a canvas and a 17 year old Hasidic kid showed up wanting to canvas - I sent him out with two exerienced canvassers (one wearing a Keffiyah) and he came back and had had the best time. We tried to give him merch but he said he'd only have to hide it at home. But he came back multiple times, and it was incredibly moving.

hero.name: Kathy
hero.text: My first canvassing experience was with a 90+ year old black woman in Brooklyn. This was in January 2025, so no one knew who Zohran was (especially in Brooklyn) and we discussed Eric Adams and then Zohran and his policies. She was extremely disappointed in Eric Adams and was not hopeful for the future of the city for upcoming generations. She was bed ridden due to her age and had a caretaker full time. She also lived in a rent stabalized apartment that she'd lived in for decades. After we discussed Zohran, she thanked us for coming by and commented on the importance of what we were doing. She had been alive during a time when she did not have the legal right to vote, so she now makes a point to vote in every single election, no matter what. But she still finds it hard to learn about every candidate and said when she was watching news coverage, there was nothing about Zohran - but there was content about many other candidates (including Cuomo who I believe was not even officially in the race at this point). She was very happy we came by and told us to keep it up. For it being my first time ever canvassing, it really cemented how meaningful it can be to someone. I felt incredibly lucky to have met her and be able to talk to her - someone I otherwise would probably never have interacted with. It made me so deeply appreciative of the experience and made me feel like I was making an actual difference even so early on in the campaign.
